question_text: Two identical metal blocks are sliding across different surfaces at the same initial speed. Block A slides on ice (friction coefficient 0.02) and Block B slides on sandpaper (friction coefficient 0.4). Both blocks have a mass of 5 kg. Explain which block will experience a greater friction force and calculate how many times greater it is. Why might someone use sandpaper instead of ice to stop a sliding object?
correct_answer: Block B experiences greater friction. The friction force on Block A is approximately 1 N (0.02 × 5 × 10 = 1 N) and on Block B is approximately 20 N (0.4 × 5 × 10 = 20 N), so Block B's friction is 20 times greater. Sandpaper provides much greater friction, so it stops objects more quickly and effectively.
explanation: Friction depends on the friction coefficient and the normal force. Even though both blocks are identical and on a horizontal surface, the sandpaper has a much higher friction coefficient than ice. This is why sandpaper is useful for safety—it creates stronger friction to slow down or stop sliding objects quickly.
